Title: Structure of the Mg-Chelatase Cofactor GUN4 Reveals a Novel Hand-Shaped Fold for Porphyrin Binding

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: GUN4 family porphyrin-binding protein; noncatalytic Mg-chelatase cofactor
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Chlorophyll biosynthesis intermediate; plastid-to-nucleus retrograde signaling molecule; photosynthesis-related gene regulation
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: X-ray crystallography; NMR chemical shift perturbation; porphyrin binding assays by tryptophan fluorescence quenching; site-directed mutagenesis; Mg-chelatase activity assays
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Synechocystis GUN4 is a porphyrin-binding protein with a unique hand-shaped GUN4 core domain that binds deuteroporphyrin and Mg-deuteroporphyrin. 2. GUN4 binds Mg-porphyrin analog more tightly than metal-free analog and stimulates Mg-chelatase-mediated Mg2+ insertion into porphyrin substrate. 3. Mutational analysis identified residues in the greasy palm and α6/α7-loop pocket, especially Arg214 and Ala219-linked features, as critical for porphyrin binding and Mg-chelatase stimulation, supporting a role in Mg-Proto sequestration/trafficking and photoprotection.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Promotes Mg-protoporphyrin production indirectly by stimulating Mg-chelatase activity and binding/sequestering Mg-porphyrins; likely supports chlorophyll accumulation and Mg-Proto signaling
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: In vitro assays; discussion links function to oxygen-rich conditions and bright light relevant to photoprotection
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Core experimental focus is Synechocystis GUN4 (SynGUN4/sll0558), considered the ortholog of Arabidopsis GUN4. The paper treats GUN4 as a noncatalytic cofactor rather than an enzyme or transcriptional regulator. Arabidopsis null gun4-2 is cited as chlorophyll-deficient background support, but not experimentally dissected here. GUN4 may assist product release from Mg-chelatase and shield reactive porphyrins from oxygen.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential target to improve chlorophyll biosynthesis efficiency, photoprotection, and tetrapyrrole homeostasis in photosynthetic organisms; engineering should preserve porphyrin-binding pocket and Mg-chelatase interaction surfaces
